[IMAGE: A collection of Apple devices, including several MacBook laptops and one iMac desktop computer, arranged in a semi-circular fashion on a white surface. All screens display the macOS desktop with a vibrant nature-themed wallpaper featuring a mountain landscape under a blue sky. From left to right, the devices are: a closed silver laptop, an open silver laptop, another open silver laptop, a silver iMac desktop, an open silver laptop, and a closed silver laptop.]

[IMAGE: A screenshot of a digital interface or webpage titled "FarmBeats" with the subtitle "Democratizing AI for farmers around the world." The background image shows a lush green agricultural field with rows of crops under a bright, slightly overcast sky. Overlayed on this image are various digital elements and data points. In the upper left, a small drone icon is visible. Text labels include "MOISTURE" and "NITROGEN 19ppm." Several small icons representing different aspects of farming technology are scattered across the field, such as a tractor, a cloud, a sun, a satellite dish, a weather vane, a plant, a water droplet, a soil sample, a thermometer, and a wind turbine. There are also abstract data visualizations, including vertical bar graphs, suggesting data analysis and monitoring.]

[IMAGE: A colorful, abstract logo or icon composed of four rounded, petal-like shapes arranged in a star or clover pattern. Each petal features a smooth color gradient. The top petal transitions from yellow to orange. The right petal transitions from orange to red/pink. The bottom petal transitions from pink to purple/blue. The left petal transitions from blue to green. The center where the four petals meet is a bright, almost white, point.]

# What is Digitization?

[Page 11]
# A new class of competitors are creating disruptive dynamics

[IMAGE: A cartoon drawing of Pac-Man. Pac-Man is a yellow circle with a large wedge-shaped mouth open on its right side. [Page 16]
# What is Disruption?

[Page 17]
# The Flee or Fight Response to Profit

[CHART: Line graph titled "The Flee or Fight Response to Profit" showing the evolution of steel quality from Integrated Mills and Mini Mills over time, alongside different steel product categories.

The X-axis represents time, labeled with years: 1975, 1980, 1985, 1990, 1995.
The Y-axis represents "Steel Quality".

There are two main diagonal lines representing the quality trends:
1.  **Quality of Integrated Mills**: This line starts high on the Y-axis and slopes slightly upwards from left to right. It indicates that integrated mills consistently produce high-quality steel.
2.  **Quality of Mini-Mill Produced Steel**: This line starts low on the Y-axis in 1975 and slopes significantly upwards, crossing the "Quality of Integrated Mills" line around 1987-1988. This indicates a rapid improvement in the quality of steel produced by mini mills over time.

There are also five horizontal lines, each labeled with a steel product type and a percentage, representing different quality tiers. These lines are generally parallel and indicate the quality level required for each product. From highest quality to lowest:
*   **Sheet Steel**: Located at the highest quality level, associated with "25-30%".
*   **Structural Steel**: Below Sheet Steel, associated with "18%".
*   **Angle Iron: Bars and Rods**: Below Structural Steel, associated with "12%".
*   **Rebar**: At the lowest quality level shown, associated with "7%".

The chart illustrates that in 1975, mini mills primarily produced lower quality steel like Rebar (7%), while integrated mills produced all qualities, including Sheet Steel (25-30%). By 1995, the quality of mini-mill produced steel had significantly improved, surpassing the quality level of integrated mills, suggesting mini mills were capable of producing higher quality products that were previously exclusive to integrated mills.]

[Page 18]
# The Flee or Fight Response to Profit

[CHART: Line graph titled "The Flee or Fight Response to Profit" showing "Steel Quality" on the Y-axis against time (1975-1995) on the X-axis.

The chart features four horizontal white lines representing different car segments or quality tiers:
*   **Topmost:** Luxury Sedan / Premium SUV
*   **Second from top:** Premium Sedan / Entry SUV
*   **Third from top:** Premium Hatchback / Entry Sedan
*   **Bottommost:** Budget Hatchback

These horizontal lines suggest a relatively stable quality benchmark for each segment over the depicted time period.

Three diagonal lines, representing different groups of car manufacturers, show their "Steel Quality" trajectory over time:
*   **Top diagonal line (green):** Labeled "BMW, Benz, Lexus". This line starts around 1985 at a quality level slightly above the "Premium Sedan / Entry SUV" segment and rises steeply, surpassing the "Luxury Sedan / Premium SUV" segment's implied quality level by 1995.
*   **Middle diagonal line (white):** Labeled "GM, Ford --- Acura, Genesis". This line starts around 1975 at a quality level slightly above the "Premium Hatchback / Entry Sedan" segment and rises steadily, reaching a quality level between the "Premium Sedan / Entry SUV" and "Luxury Sedan / Premium SUV" segments by 1995.
*   **Bottom diagonal line (white):** Labeled "Toyota, Kia, Hyundai". This line starts around 1975 at a quality level slightly above the "Budget Hatchback" segment and rises steadily, reaching a quality level between the "Premium Hatchback / Entry Sedan" and "Premium Sedan / Entry SUV" segments by 1995.]

[Page 19]
# Why do incumbents ignore?

[Page 20]
# How Disruptions Occur

[CHART: A 2D line graph titled "How Disruptions Occur" with "Performance" on the Y-axis and "Time" on the X-axis. A single white line slopes upwards from the bottom-left to the top-right of the graph, representing increasing performance over time. An arrow points from the line downwards to a text label that reads "Performance improvement required by mainstream market".]

[Page 21]
# How Disruptions Occur

[CHART: A 2D line graph titled "How Disruptions Occur" with "Performance" on the Y-axis and "Time" on the X-axis.

There are two main elements plotted on the graph:
1.  **Performance improvement required by mainstream market**: Represented by an upward-sloping solid white line with an arrow pointing to the right, indicating increasing performance over time. A vertical arrow points from the text "Performance improvement required by mainstream market" to this line.
2.  **Current performance of potentially disruptive innovation**: Represented by a single white dot with an arrow pointing to the right, indicating its position on the graph. This dot is located significantly below the "Performance improvement required by mainstream market" line and to the left, suggesting lower performance at an earlier point in time. The text "Current performance of potentially disruptive innovation" points to this dot.]

[Page 22]
# How Disruptions Occur

[CHART: A 2D line graph illustrating how disruptions occur, with "Time" on the X-axis and "Performance" on the Y-axis.
There are three key elements depicted:
1.  **Performance improvement required by mainstream market**: Represented by an upward-sloping straight line, starting at a moderate performance level and increasing gradually over time. An arrow points from this line to the label "Performance improvement required by mainstream market".
2.  **Current performance of potentially disruptive innovation**: Represented by a single point in the lower-left quadrant of the graph, indicating a low initial performance level at an early point in time. An arrow points from this point to the label "Current performance of potentially disruptive innovation".
3.  **Expected trajectory of performance improvement**: Represented by a much steeper upward-sloping line that originates from the "Current performance of potentially disruptive innovation" point. This line crosses the "Performance improvement required by mainstream market" line, indicating that the disruptive innovation's performance rapidly improves and eventually surpasses the requirements of the mainstream market. An arrow points from this line to the label "Expected trajectory of performance improvement".]

[Page 23]
# Why do incumbents ignore?

*   Disruption is a process – Not one fixed point – which is why incumbents ignore
*   Disruptors often build business models that are different from those of the incumbents
*   Incumbents chasing higher profits do not respond vigorously
*   Disruptors move upmarket – delivering performance that incumbent's mainstream customers require – in the process 'Over Serve'
*   Mainstream customers adopt disruptors offerings in volume

[Page 24]
# Why do incumbents ignore?

* Incumbents experience – The Innovator's Dilemma
* Incumbents tend to measure the performance of the Disruptor through their own lens

[Page 25]
Disruptive Innovation?

[Page 26]
# Disruptive Innovation – has a specific usage

*   A seemingly sub par offering – fewer resources – challenges an established incumbent business
*   Incumbents focus on improving their products for their most demanding customers – exceed / ignore needs
*   Entrants target these overserved segments – delivering value usually at a lower price
*   Entrants can also target a ‘new’ / unserved market

[Page 27]
# Disruptive Innovation

*   Disruptive Innovations originate in the low-end or new-market footholds
*   Incumbents – Overfit or Ignore
*   Disruptors – Plug the gap or change non consumers to consumers

[Page 28]
# Think of...

*   Music – From Vinyl – Cassette – CD – IPod – Streaming
*   Books – From physical experience – Digital Experience
*   FinTech – Paper Transaction – Digital Transactions

[Page 29]
Each one of these was possible
because of certain technological
invariants

[Page 30]
# Three Technological Invariants

*   **Digitization** – Use of technology to separate information from the physical artifact
*   **Computing Power** – Allows for vast quantities of information to be stored and accessed at relatively low costs
*   **Modularity** – Ensures several devices, applications work seamlessly with one another. This results in wide distribution

[Page 31]
# Disruptive Innovations

Disruptive innovations transform expensive or complicated products into simpler and more affordable ones.

[DIAGRAM: A Venn diagram consisting of three concentric circles, representing different generations of computing devices and their associated pricing and gross margins.
The outermost and largest circle is labeled "Mainframes - P - $2M GM - $1.22M".
The middle circle, nested within the Mainframes circle, is labeled "PC / Laptops P - $2K GM - $700".
The innermost and smallest circle, nested within the PC / Laptops circle, is labeled "Smartphones P - $200 GM - $80".
The diagram illustrates how each successive innovation (from mainframes to PCs/laptops to smartphones) has significantly reduced the product price (P) and gross margin (GM), making technology more accessible and affordable.]

[Page 32]
# Other Forms of Innovation

[Page 33]
Sustaining Innovation
Efficiency Innovation

[Page 34]
# What is Sustaining Innovation?

[Page 35]
# Value Networks And The Pursuit of Innovation

[DIAGRAM: A rectangular box with a white outline contains a network diagram. In the center, "FIRM" is positioned. To the left of "FIRM" is "Customers", and to the right is "Suppliers". A double-headed arrow connects "Customers" and "FIRM", indicating a two-way relationship. Another double-headed arrow connects "FIRM" and "Suppliers", also indicating a two-way relationship. Below "FIRM", "Competitors" is positioned, and a double-headed arrow connects "FIRM" and "Competitors", indicating a two-way relationship.]

[Page 36]
# Value Networks And The Pursuit of Innovation

[DIAGRAM: A central diagram illustrating a "Value Network" with a title "Value Networks And The Pursuit of Innovation".

The core of the diagram is a large white rounded rectangle that encloses four entities:
- **FIRM**: Positioned centrally.
- **Customers**: Positioned to the left of FIRM, connected to FIRM by a double-headed horizontal arrow.
- **Suppliers**: Positioned to the right of FIRM, connected to FIRM by a double-headed horizontal arrow.
- **Competitors**: Positioned below FIRM, connected to FIRM by a single vertical arrow pointing downwards from FIRM to Competitors.

Two large, light blue arrows originate from the bottom corners of the main rounded rectangle and point downwards to two separate text boxes:

1.  **Left Text Box**: Located below the left side of the main diagram, connected by a large light blue arrow originating from the bottom-left of the main rectangle.
    Text: "Enhance and preserve the existing value network through *sustaining innovations*"

2.  **Right Text Box**: Located below the right side of the main diagram, connected by a large light blue arrow originating from the bottom-right of the main rectangle.
    Text: "Create and lead a new value network through *disruptive innovations*"
]

[Page 37]
Make existing products / value network better –
Make better smartphones, better laptops

[Page 38]
# What is Efficiency Innovation?

[Page 39]
Reduce cost / time - example?

[Page 40]
# Is it really disruptive?

*   Does the product target non consumers or people who are overserved by an incumbent's existing offering?
*   Is the offering NOT as good as an incumbent’s existing offering as judged by historical measures of performance?
*   Is the innovation simpler to use, more convenient or more affordable than the incumbent's existing offering?

[Page 41]
# Is it really disruptive?

*   Does the offering have a technological enabler that can carry it upmarket and allow it to improve?
*   Are existing providers motivated to ignore the new innovation and are not threatened at the outset?
*   Is a business model innovation possible to ensure sustainability?

[Page 42]
Let us dive deeper with an example
you are all living through

[Page 43]
# Disrupting MBA

[Page 44]
MBA programs as we know them be
completely disrupted in the next 5
years

[Page 45]
Is there any evidence that the market is being overserved?

[Page 46]
What product is serving them?

ISB Online / ISBx

# University of Iowa's Tippie College to End Full-Time M.B.A. Program

It will graduate final class of two-year M.B.A. students in 2019 as it faces a 'shrinking market'

[IMAGE: A modern, multi-story building with large windows, featuring a prominent sign that reads "Tippie College of Business". The building has a light-colored facade and is surrounded by green landscaping and a paved area.]

[IMAGE: An advertisement box with a dark background. The main text in large white letters says "END MULTI-CLOUD COMPLEXITY". Below that, in smaller white text, it reads "Lower costs and gain greater control of deployments." At the bottom, there's a red button with white text "LEARN MORE". Below the text, there's an image of a person's hands, one holding a smartphone displaying a graph, and the other hand gesturing.]

# Why Business Schools Are Shutting Down Their MBA Programs

# U of I to end on-campus MBA classes

The Gies College of Business in Urbana-Champaign will direct more resources to its fast-growing online MBA program, among others. “The iMBA is the right format for the times," according to the dean.

The University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign plans to stop offering on-campus MBA studies, opting instead to direct resources to its fast-growing online MBA program, among others.

[Page 49]
# Is it really disruptive?

*   Does the product target non consumers or people who are overserved by an incumbent's existing offering?
*   Is the offering NOT as good as an incumbent’s existing offering as judged by historical measures of performance?
*   Is the innovation simpler to use, more convenient or more affordable than the incumbent's existing offering?

[Page 50]
# Is it really disruptive?

*   Does the offering have a technological enabler that can carry it upmarket and allow it to improve?
*   Are existing providers motivated to ignore the new innovation and are not threatened at the outset?
*   Is a business model innovation possible to ensure sustainability?

[Page 51]
That's All Folks!
